Jeff Larkin's Visit and The Possible Futures of Software Engineering

02-23-2026

This past week in my AI class, we had Jeff Larkin come in as a guest speaker. Jeff is the director of high-performance computing at NVIDIA, and his presentation was one of the more thought-provoking conversations we've had in this class. To me, the most interesting topic that Jeff covered was the real world applications of AI. He showed us a system that NVIDIA has been developing that uses AI to predict many different weather forecast possibilities. Each one is assigned a likelihood; after looking at the likelihood of each storm path, the most likely ones can be run through the real, accurate system that meteorologists use. The benefit of using AI is that you can test more possibiliies with less compute time. I've always thought of AI as a very powerful computational tool, but seeing the real world potential revealed a new sense of value to me, regarding these tools


What Jeff Talked About

Jeff's background is in high-performance computing, which at its core is about squeezing every bit of performance out of hardware. Before working at NVIDIA, that looked like optimizing scientific simulations for supercomputers. Now, it's largely about making sure AI workloads run efficiently on GPU clusters. One thing that struck me was how he described the shift in who uses HPC. It used to be a niche field—researchers at national labs, academics running physics simulations. Today, the demand for that same kind of parallel computing knowledge has exploded because of AI training runs and inference at scale.


He was also candid about the challenges. Scaling GPU clusters introduces an enormous number of engineering problems that aren't glamorous—memory bandwidth, network interconnects, thermal management. The work that keeps a large AI training run from failing halfway through isn't the stuff that makes headlines, but it's what actually makes the technology work. That was a useful reminder that "AI engineering" isn't just prompt engineering and fine-tuning. There's a deep stack of infrastructure work underneath it.


The Possible Futures of Software Engineering

The part of Jeff's talk I've been thinking about most is when he talked about where software engineering is going. His perspective, coming from someone who works at the hardware level, was different from what I usually hear. A lot of the conversation in my classes is about how AI will write code, and what that means for developers. Jeff's framing was more about specialization. As AI handles more of the surface-level implementation work, the engineers who understand what's happening at the hardware level— memory hierarchies, parallelism, data movement—are going to become more valuable, not less.


That's a future I find both exciting and a little uncomfortable. It's exciting because it suggests that deep technical knowledge still matters, and probably matters more than it does now if the baseline tasks are automated away. It's uncomfortable because that kind of specialization takes a long time to develop, and I'm not sure our curriculum is designed to produce it. Most CS programs, including mine, give you enough exposure to systems concepts to be dangerous, but not enough to actually optimize a CUDA kernel or reason confidently about memory access patterns on a modern GPU.


There's also the question of what "software engineering" even means going forward. Jeff described a model where engineers increasingly work at the level of systems design and correctness verification, while AI handles the code generation in between. That doesn't eliminate the engineer—it changes what the engineer needs to be good at. The bottleneck shifts from writing code to specifying problems well, understanding the hardware constraints, and catching the cases where the generated code is subtly wrong. None of those are easy skills. They're just different from what we've been training for.


My Takeaway

The visit reinforced something I've been turning over since the start of this class: the engineers who are going to be most useful in ten years are the ones who understand the full stack, not just the layer they happen to work in. Jeff made his career by understanding hardware deeply enough to write software that actually takes advantage of it. That mindset—go one level deeper than you need to—seems like good advice regardless of how much AI changes the surface of the job.

I also appreciated that he was honest about uncertainty. Nobody really knows what this all looks like in ten years, including the people building the hardware that's driving it. That's both a frustrating and freeing thing to hear from someone in his position. Frustrating because I'd love a clear answer. Freeing because it means there's still room to help figure it out.
